12   Which of the following statements about polynomials f and g is/are true?
                                                   x               x
         I    If f(x) ≥ g(x) for all x ≥ 0, then    0
                                                        f(t) dt ≥   0
                                                                         g(t) dt for all x ≥ 0.

         II   If f(x) ≥ g(x) for all x ≥ 0, then f  (x) ≥ g (x) for all x ≥ 0.

         III If f  (x) ≥ g (x) for all x ≥ 0, then f(x) ≥ g(x) for all x ≥ 0.

     A none of them

     B I only

     C II only

     D III only

     E I and II only

     F I and III only

     G II and III only

     H I, II and III

stem.rawText:
Which of the following statements about polynomials f and g is/are true?
                                                   Cx               Cx
         I    If f(x) ≥ g(x) for all x ≥ 0, then    0
                                                        f(t) dt ≥   0
                                                                         g(t) dt for all x ≥ 0.

         II   If f(x) ≥ g(x) for all x ≥ 0, then f  (x) ≥ g (x) for all x ≥ 0.

         III If f  (x) ≥ g (x) for all x ≥ 0, then f(x) ≥ g(x) for all x ≥ 0.

solution.rawText:
Rx
I    We can think of the integral 0 f(t) dt as being the (signed) area under the graph of y = f(x)
     from 0 to x. Since f(x) ≥ g(x) for all x ≥ 0, the area under the graph of y = f(x) will also
     be greater than that under the graph of y = g(x), so this statement is true.
     You might be concerned about values of x for which g(x) is negative. We could therefore
     demonstrate this algebraically as well.
     We have                  Z x               Z x               Z x
                                    f(t) dt −         g(t) dt =         (f(t) − g(t)) dt.
                               0                 0                 0
     Since f(t) − g(t) ≥ 0 for all x ≥ 0, we are integrating a non-negative
                                                                  Rx        function,
                                                                              Rx      and so the
     integral must be non-negative, that is to say, we must have 0 f(t) dt ≥ 0 g(t) dt.

II   It is not clear why f(x) ≥ g(x) should imply that the gradient (derivative) of f(x) is greater
     than that of g(x), and it is relatively straightforward to find a counterexample. Here is one:

                                                         y
                                                                   f(x) = (x − 1)2




                                                                       g(x) = 0     x
                                                     0


     In this example, f(x) ≥ g(x) for all x, but f ′ (0) < 0 while g′ (x) = 0 for all x.

III This is not necessarily true, as we could add a constant to g(x) without changing g′ (x). For
    example, we could have f ′ (x) = g′ (x) = 1 for all x, but f(x) = x while g(x) = x + 1, so
    f(x) < g(x) for all x.

The correct option is therefore B.
